abstract: GROMACS has matured over the last two decades into one of the most widely used packages for biomolecular simulation, evolving from a lean, efficient engine for small systems into a sophisticated heterogeneous framework designed for the world’s largest supercomputers. In this work, we detail the strategies employed to achieve this scalability, focusing on multi-level parallelization and a rigorous commitment to absolute performance. Release 4.6 of GROMACS introduces SIMD acceleration across diverse architectures, GPU offloading, and a hybrid parallelization model using OpenMP and MPI to manage communication both within and between nodes. This push for hardware acceleration necessitated a fundamental re-evaluation of core molecular simulation algorithms, particularly neighbor-searching techniques. We further explore the challenges associated with reaching exascale performance—emphasizing the need for fine-grained task parallelism—and describe the modernization of our software management practices, including peer review and continuous integration, which are essential for maintaining a codebase of this complexity.
